original_hypothesis.title: VeToken Delegation Hijacking via Unrestricted stake() Receiver Parameter
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: AgentVeToken.stake() allows any caller to specify an arbitrary `receiver` for minted veTokens and forcibly overrides that receiver's entire delegation to a caller-chosen `delegatee`. An attacker can call stake(1, victim, attackerValidator) to redirect ALL of victim's accumulated voting power to their controlled validator, at the cost of only 1 LP token.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The _delegate(receiver, delegatee) call operates on the FULL balance of receiver, not just the newly minted amount. This enables wholesale governance takeover for minimal cost.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) called with user-supplied receiver without verifying msg.sender == receiver; overrides entire balance delegation

preconditions: Attacker holds >= 1 LP token (the staking asset)
Victim has existing veToken balance with delegation to a different validator

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _delegate(receiver, delegatee) in stake() overrides delegation for receiver's ENTIRE balance, not just the new amount
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker calls stake(1, victim, attackerDelegatee) to redirect victim's full voting power
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker gains victim's entire governance voting power for 1 LP token; can control DAO proposals, set DAO address, change agent metadata

economic_questions: What is the total staked value delegable per agent?
Can the attacker cascade this across multiple victims to gain DAO supermajority?
Can the attacker use DAO control to call setDAO and permanently hijack governance?
validation_requirements: Confirm _delegate overwrites entire balance delegation via OZ ERC20VotesUpgradeable
Test atomic attack: stake(1, victim, attacker) in single tx
Verify no hidden check on receiver == msg.sender in _update or _delegate

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Full governance control over agent worth potentially millions in token/LP value

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Attacker needs minimal LP tokens obtainable from Uniswap
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Any time after victim has staked and delegated
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: No market impact; pure governance manipulation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Any veToken holder with existing delegation
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All delegated governance power per agent

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm voting power transfer is immediate and total

validation_result.mechanism: unrestricted delegation override via receiver parameter
validation_result.impact_summary: Any caller can hijack any veToken holder's entire voting power delegation by calling stake(1, victim, attackerDelegatee) at the cost of a single LP token, redirecting all accumulated governance power.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The AgentVeToken.stake() function accepts user-supplied `receiver` and `delegatee` parameters without verifying that msg.sender == receiver. After minting tokens to the receiver, it calls _delegate(receiver, delegatee), which is an OpenZeppelin ERC20VotesUpgradeable internal function that operates on the FULL balance of the account, not just the newly minted amount. This means an attacker can spend 1 LP token to forcibly redirect a victim's entire accumulated voting power to an attacker-controlled delegatee. There is no check anywhere in the call chain (stake -> _mint -> _update -> _delegate) that restricts who can change another user's delegation. The _update override in AgentVeToken simply calls super._update with no additional guards. The ERC20Votes wrapper's _delegate also just calls super._delegate and pushes to checkpoints with no sender validation.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The vulnerability is clearly present in AgentVeToken.sol lines 49-73. The stake() function takes (uint256 amount, address receiver, address delegatee) as public parameters with no access control on receiver. Line 70: `_delegate(receiver, delegatee)` operates on the full balance of receiver. The ERC20Votes.sol override at lines 10-13 calls super._delegate(account, delegatee) which moves ALL voting power. No msg.sender == receiver check exists anywhere in the code path.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: No check that msg.sender == receiver before calling _delegate(receiver, delegatee). The _delegate call overrides the ENTIRE delegation for receiver's full balance, not just the newly minted amount. An attacker can specify any victim as receiver and redirect all their voting power.

The _delegate override passes through to ERC20VotesUpgradeable._delegate which moves ALL voting power of 'account' from old delegatee to new delegatee. Combined with the unrestricted stake() caller, this enables complete voting power theft.

calling_sequence: 4. Balance/allowance checks pass for attacker's 1 LP token
7. safeTransferFrom pulls 1 LP token from attacker
9. _delegate(victim, attackerDelegatee) - CRITICAL: overrides victim's ENTIRE delegation
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is stake() -> _delegate(receiver, delegatee) at AgentVeToken.sol:71. The _delegate call in ERC20Votes.sol:11 calls super._delegate(account, delegatee) which is OZ ERC20VotesUpgradeable._delegate. This function: (a) looks up the old delegatee for 'account', (b) moves ALL votes from old delegatee to new delegatee based on account's FULL balance, (c) stores new delegatee. Since receiver is attacker-controlled and there is no msg.sender == receiver check, the attacker controls whose delegation gets overridden.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment configuration affects this vulnerability. The canStake parameter is set per-agent during executeApplication and can be toggled by founder via setCanStake.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack is fully validated: (1) Alice stakes 1000 LP tokens into a public agent's veToken, delegating to ValidatorA. Alice has 1000 veTokens delegated to ValidatorA. (2) Bob acquires 1 LP token from Uniswap (negligible cost). (3) Bob approves the veToken contract for 1 LP token. (4) Bob calls stake(1, Alice, MaliciousValidator). (5) The function mints 1 veToken to Alice (now 1001), then calls _delegate(Alice, MaliciousValidator), which moves ALL 1001 votes from ValidatorA to MaliciousValidator. (6) Bob spent 1 LP token but redirected Alice's entire 1001 voting power. This can be repeated for every staker in the system.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. Any public agent's governance can be completely hijacked at negligible cost. An attacker can redirect ALL stakers' voting power to a controlled validator in a single transaction per victim. With majority voting power, the attacker can pass DAO proposals to call setDAO (AgentNftV2.sol) replacing the DAO with an attacker-controlled contract, permanently hijacking agent governance. This enables changing tokenURI, coreTypes, and all DAO-gated operations. The attack cost is O(n) LP tokens where n is the number of victims, but each token can be as small as 1 wei of LP.
recommended_mitigations: Add require(receiver == msg.sender, 'Cannot delegate for others') in the stake() function
Alternatively, separate staking from delegation: only mint to receiver in stake(), and require a separate delegate() call that can only be made by the token holder themselves
If third-party staking-on-behalf is desired, do NOT call _delegate when receiver != msg.sender, or only delegate for the newly minted amount (requires custom delegation logic)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AgentVeToken with a mock LP token and AgentNft. 2. Alice stakes 1000 LP tokens delegating to ValidatorA. 3. Verify Alice's votes are with ValidatorA via getVotes(). 4. Bob stakes 1 LP token with receiver=Alice, delegatee=BobValidator. 5. Verify ALL votes (1001) are now with BobValidator and ValidatorA has 0 votes. 6. Confirm Bob only spent 1 LP token.
uncertainties: The exact OZ ERC20VotesUpgradeable._delegate implementation is not in scope, but standard OZ behavior is well-documented: it moves the full balance's voting power from old to new delegatee
additional_testing_needed: Verify the attack works end-to-end with a Foundry/Hardhat test
Test cascading the attack across multiple victims to achieve DAO majority
Test whether the attacker can then pass a setDAO proposal to permanently hijack governance
